chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Solanine is heavily concentrated in the green parts of nightshade plants, such as unripe fruits, green potato skins, potato sprouts (eyes), leaves, and stems1.... Ripe fruits and cooked/peeled potatoes contain only trace amounts and are generally tolerable
chemicals.description: in cats: These toxins harm cats through several severe systemic mechanisms: ◦ Cytotoxic effect: Solanine interacts with the phospholipids in cell membranes, increasing permeability and causing cell destruction. This particularly damages the intestinal epithelial cells, causing severe gastrointestinal irritation, inflammation, vomiting, and diarrhea3. ◦ Neurological disruption: It inhibits acetylch
chemicals.name: Solanine and α-chaconine, which are glycoalkaloid neurotoxins

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Tomatine is highest in concentration in the leaves, stems, and unripe (green) tomatoes912. Fully ripe red tomatoes contain negligible levels of tomatine and pose no meaningful threat9.... It is generally considered slightly less toxic than solanine, requiring very large doses to cause severe systemic illness
chemicals.description: in cats: Tomatine acts as a natural defense mechanism against pests913. Similar to solanine, cats lack the specific enzymes required to efficiently break this compound down13. Ingestion leads to gastrointestinal distress, lethargy, and in extreme cases, heart rate irregularities
chemicals.name: Tomatine, a glycoalkaloid closely related to solanine

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: While mild bell peppers contain only small amounts and are relatively harmless, capsaicin is heavily concentrated in spicy pepper varieties, making them particularly dangerous and irritating to pets
chemicals.description: in cats: Capsaicin is highly irritating to the mucous membranes. If ingested, it can lead to gastroenteritis, coughing, hypersalivation, and watery eyes
chemicals.name: Capsaicin

description: Nightshade (Solanum nigrum and related Solanum species), also called Black Nightshade or Common Nightshade, is a toxic plant for cats found in gardens, roadsides, and as a weed worldwide. Like all members of the nightshade family (Solanaceae), it contains steroidal glycoalkaloids — primarily solanine and solasonine — with the highest concentrations in unripe berries, leaves, and stems.
emergencyNote: Oxygen Therapy, Mechanical Ventilation, and Anticonvulsant Medications
name: Nightshade
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: solanine and solasonine (steroidal glycoalkaloids); unripe green berries and green plant parts most concentrated; ripe black berries slightly less toxic
Mechanism: glycoalkaloids inhibit acetylcholinesterase (disrupting nerve signalling) and disrupt cell membranes, causing GI and neurological effects
Symptoms: drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, lethargy, weakness; severe ingestion may cause tremors, bradycardia, or CNS depression
Unripe berries are the greatest risk — they resemble edible berries and may attract curious cats
Contact your vet if your cat ingests any part of the plant, especially unripe berries

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea (sometimes bloody), and significant abdominal pain
symptoms.notes: Solanine interacts directly with the cell membranes, specifically causing destruction, irritation, and inflammation to the intestinal epithelial cells7. This leads to violent lower gastrointestinal problems and tense abdominal walls46. 2. Hypersalivation and Mucous Membrane Irritation

symptoms.name: Excessive drooling (hypersalivation), burning sensation, and coughing
symptoms.notes: This is caused by the direct irritating effect of the plant material on the mucous membranes, as well as the overstimulation of the parasympathetic nervous system4.... 3. Neurological Abnormalities (Lethargy, Tremors, Seizures, and Ataxia)

symptoms.name: Lethargy, depression, confusion, dilated pupils, ataxia (loss of coordination), muscle tremors, stupor, and seizures
symptoms.notes: Solanine inhibits the enzyme acetylcholinesterase in the brain and impairs mitochondrial function, severely disrupting the central nervous system3.... Cats are exceptionally sensitive to these toxins because their specific liver metabolism (lack of glucuronidation) slows detoxification, resulting in
symptoms.onset: 2–24 hours (typically follows gastrointestinal symptoms as the toxin is absorbed into the bloodstrea

symptoms.name: Abnormally slow heart rate (bradycardia) and circulatory failure
symptoms.notes: This occurs at higher toxic doses due to the severe overstimulation of the parasympathetic nervous system59. If left untreated, circulatory failure can lead to death4. 5. Hemolysis, Anemia, and Kidney Damage

symptoms.name: Hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells), resulting in anemia, jaundice (icterus), and hemoglobinuria (dark red or brown urine)
symptoms.notes: In severe cases, the toxins destroy the red blood cells4. The released hemoglobin from this destruction is nephrotoxic and can subsequently damage the kidneys, contributing to acute kidney failure3.... 6. Respiratory Paralysis

symptoms.name: Difficulty breathing progressing to respiratory paralysis
symptoms.notes: In particularly severe, untreated cases of nightshade poisoning, the ultimate cause of death is often respiratory paralysis and cardiovascular collapse
symptoms.onset: 2–24 hours (usually in the terminal stages of severe poisoning)

treatments.description: The veterinarian will provide supplemental oxygen or utilize mechanical ventilation to support breathing. Seizures are actively controlled using anticonvulsant medications such as benzodiazepines or barbiturates
treatments.name: Oxygen Therapy, Mechanical Ventilation, and Anticonvulsant Medications
treatments.notes: This is the most critical, life-saving intervention if the cat presents in the severe, terminal stages of poisoning, which can cause severe respiratory depression, respiratory paralysis, and active seizures12. 2. Gastrointestinal Decontamination

treatments.description: The veterinarian will remove the toxin from the stomach by medically inducing vomiting (typically using xylazine in cats) or by performing a gastric lavage (stomach pumping) under anesthesia3. Following this, activated charcoal (1–4 g/kg body weight) is administered to bind to any unabsorbed solanine in the gastrointestinal tract and pass it safely
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Vomiting), Gastric Lavage, and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: Inducing vomiting should only be performed if the ingestion was very recent (within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat has absent or only mild symptoms3. In cases of severe poisoning, the activated charcoal administration may need to be repeated multiple times at 4- to 6-hour intervals1. 3. Fluid Therapy and

treatments.description: IV fluids are administered to correct dehydration caused by severe vomiting and diarrhea, stimulate diuresis (urine production), and promote the excretion of the toxins
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy with Crystalloid Solutions
treatments.notes: If the cat's blood work or urine shows signs of hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells), the veterinarian must increase the IV infusion rate. This is critical to ensure adequate blood flow (perfusion) to the kidneys and prevent the released hemoglobin from causing acute kidney failure1. 4. Target

treatments.description: The veterinarian will administer atropine to partially compensate for the effects of the toxin on the brain and nervous system
treatments.name: Atropine Administration
treatments.notes: There is no direct, specific antidote for solanine poisoning; however, atropine is highly effective at treating the specific cholinergic symptoms of the poisoning, such as an abnormally slow heart rate (bradycardia) and excessive drooling14. 5. Ongoing Monitoring and Dietary Aftercare

treatments.description: The cat's vital parameters, blood counts, and kidney functions will be continuously monitored throughout the hospital stay1. Upon returning home, the cat is fed a gentle, easily digestible diet for 3 to 5 days
treatments.name: Organ Monitoring and Bland Diet
treatments.notes: Because kidney and liver damage can occur with a delay following nightshade poisoning, aftercare requires regular veterinary monitoring of liver and kidney functions for several weeks after the acute phase has passed
